summary: Dan Shapiro provides a detailed parts list and guide for building a high-performance RC plane for under $100. The post accompanies his appearance on the Gweek podcast with Mark Frauenfelder, where they discussed Robot Turtles, maker culture, and RC flying. Shapiro walks through every component needed — from the Turnigy Bonsai airframe to motors, props, batteries, servos, and a keychain camera — with prices and practical tips. He also touches on more advanced setups like FPV rigs and long-endurance UAVs, and shares aerial footage from flights over Maui.
key_points: A fully functional, overpowered RC plane with aerial video capability can be built for under $100 in parts
Elevon-style planes (roll + pitch, no rudder) are more fun and intuitive than rudder-based designs
Upgrading the motor beyond the plane's spec creates dramatically better performance at minimal cost
Practicing on an RC simulator before flying prevents destroying your investment on the first outing
money_quotes: A lightweight, insanely powerful plane that can fold flat in your suitcase and shoot screaming aerial videos for $99.78.
It seems counterintuitive to have roll control but no yaw control, but trust me, it's much more fun that way.
The first got caught up in surprisingly strong winds off the coast of Aruba and was lost at sea.
Note that it's about twice as beefy as what the plane's designed for so you'll need a bigger battery too to offset it.
For a few hundred bucks more, you can upgrade to a cheap UAV.
key_insight: With under $100 in commodity hobby parts and some basic soldering skills, anyone can build a high-performance RC plane capable of shooting aerial video.
